title: Presigned Browser Uploads, POST Policies, and CORS · Execution Trace: Upload Session Intent
summary: A browser receives a broad week-long upload URL, changes signed headers, overwrites a shared key, and mistakes CORS success for storage authorization. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the upload session intent increment in least-privilege direct-upload session contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Create a one-object upload intent with tenant, generated key, expected size, type, checksum, expiry, status, and replay policy before signing.
Abort known failures, inventory abandoned uploads, and configure AbortIncompleteMultipartUpload lifecycle cleanup where supported.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Upload Session Intent · Theory Upload Session Intent
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 9198 during a regional dependency incident. Build deterministic Go evidence to finalize a direct upload against intent while separating CORS from IAM authorization; cap bytes, time, attempts, concurrency, and spend before starting work; then change cache and retrieval cost assumptions and defend the new budget result. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Create a one-object upload intent with tenant, generated key, expected size, type, checksum, expiry, status, and replay policy before signing.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Upload Session Intent contract for the supplied data.
